titleStmt.title: Letter from John Crozier to Edward Carson, 26 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Baptist Crozier

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: John Baptist Crozier (1853–1920) to Edward Carson (1854-1935). Carson was a prominent Unionist Politician. Originally a practicing solicitor from Dublin, Carson's opposition to the proposed third Home Rule bill made him the figure head of Unionism throughout the island. He was the centerpiece of the speaking tour that culminated in Ulster Day (28 September 1912), when just under half a million signed Ulster's solemn league and covenant pledging to use ‘all means which may be found necessary to defeat the present conspiracy to set up a home rule parliament in Dublin’. Carson inaugurated the Larne gunrunning in 1914 which armed the UVF, many of whom Carson would encourage to join the British Army throughout the First World War. Crozier was a Church of Ireland clergyman who served as Archbishop of Armagh and Primate of All Ireland from 1911–1920.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

correspAction.placeName: The Palace Armagh, Co. Armagh, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D1507/A/17/26 PAGE 1 OF 2 100 The Palace,
Armagh. June 26.1916 My Dear Ned Many thanks for
yours. The Bishops of course
at their meeting looked at
the question simply from the
standpoint of it's effect on the
Church all over Ireland As regards the Covenant
I think it has been faithfully
kept when the 3 exempted
Counties asquiesced and
released their comrades.
I myself was a Co Cavan
Covenanter where I signed
it in company with my
own Tenants & others.
And I feel sure that if
I had been present at
your meeting I could not
have opposed as a
Covenanter the decision
come to, in fact I always
felt that Lord Farnham,
Saunderson, Burrowes,
& Johnston had a right
to speak for the County
& they refused to take
the responsibility of
objecting. But as Bishops of the Church
we protest against the
flagrant breach of faith and
honour on the part of the
Government. We protest,
as representing the best part
of the population all over
Ireland, against the Church
of Ireland not having been
consulted by the Government
in such a grave matter
& above all we protest that,
as far as we are concerned,
We are utterly in the dark
as to any Imperial Necessity
for such an appalling
change of Government of
Ireland as is contemplated. I cannot but think
that the Unionist Members
of the Cabinet have been
utterly tricked and should
have taken their stand on the
impossibility of considering
any such measure
during the war crisis. This is all that the
Bishop's Resolution means
& is in no sense a criticism
on, or a condemnation of,
the action of the Ulster
Covenanters. As ever affecy John B: Armagh
D1507/A/17/26 PAGE 2 OF 2 103 Private
I may add very
definately after a
conversation now
with the Bishop of
Down that we
Northern Bishops do
feel that if Home
Rule were bound
to come here now,
that perplexing
trend the very
private mention
so far as Ulster
is concerned.
But we all feel
( i.e. Southern &
Northern Bishops
alike) that the
whole thing is a
breach of faith on the
Governments part
& that we at any
rate know of No
Imperial Necessity
J.B.a
